Title: Glutathione degradation activity of γ-glutamyl peptidase 1 manifests its dual roles in primary and secondary sulfur metabolism in Arabidopsis

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Glutathione degradation; glucosinolate and camalexin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Primary sulfur metabolism via cytosolic GSH degradation; secondary sulfur metabolism via GSH-conjugate processing in glucosinolate/camalexin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Glutathione (GSH); also GSH conjugates in glucosinolate/camalexin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Cys-Gly and Glu from GSH degradation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes the γ-glutamyl bond of glutathione to release Cys-Gly and Glu; also processes glutathione conjugates in glucosinolate and camalexin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream degradation step of glutathione; branch point shared with secondary sulfur metabolite synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly degrades target product glutathione and also processes conjugated intermediates for glucosinolate/camalexin production
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as major control enzyme for GSH degradation in mature organs under sulfur-sufficient conditions
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Glutathione serves as a major sulfur repository and redox metabolite; glucosinolates/isothiocyanates and camalexin function in defense
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GGP1 increases glutathione accumulation under normal, sulfur-deficient, and nitrogen-deficient conditions, elevates O-acetyl-L-serine under sulfur deficiency, and decreases multiple glucosinolates and isothiocyanates
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly abundant in rosette leaves; higher in mature than young leaves; among the most abundant GSH-degradation transcripts
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in rosette leaves and 2-week-old liquid-cultured plants; slightly induced by nitrogen deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sulfur deficiency; nitrogen deficiency; buthionine sulfoximine treatment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast complementation of dug2Δ/dug3Δ mutants; recombinant protein enzyme assay and kinetics; T-DNA knockout mutant metabolite profiling; double mutant analysis under BSO; qRT-PCR; widely targeted metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Widely targeted metabolomics (LC-QqQ-MS/MS); no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target to manipulate sulfur remobilization and balance between glutathione turnover and defense sulfur metabolites; editing may alter both primary sulfur recycling and glucosinolate/camalexin traits
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced glucosinolate and isothiocyanate accumulation and possible defense-related tradeoffs when GGP1 is disrupted; dual function complicates engineering
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGP1 complemented yeast dug2Δ and dug3Δ mutants, demonstrating glutathione degradation capacity. 2. Recombinant GGP1 degraded GSH in vitro but hardly degraded GSSG, γ-Glu-Cys, or γ-Glu-Ala. 3. ggp1 knockout mutants constitutively accumulated more GSH, showing that GGP1 is a major in planta GSH degradation enzyme. 4. ggp1 mutants accumulated more O-acetyl-L-serine under sulfur deficiency, consistent with altered sulfur status. 5. GGP1 disruption decreased several glucosinolates and isothiocyanates, supporting a dual role in primary and secondary sulfur metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper proposes that the GGP-mediated route is more energy efficient than the GGCT-mediated route because GGPs directly generate Glu without ATP-consuming 5-oxoproline conversion. GGP1 is also modeled structurally as accepting both GSH and GSH conjugates.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Glutathione degradation; glucosinolate and camalexin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Primary sulfur metabolism via cytosolic GSH degradation; likely auxiliary route overlapping with GGP1
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes glutathione in vitro, releasing Cys-Gly and Glu; likely also processes GSH conjugates by analogy to GGP1
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major sulfur storage and redistribution metabolite
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Recombinant GGP3 has high GSH degradation activity and is proposed to contribute with GGP1 to cytosolic GSH degradation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutively expressed; higher in mature leaves than young leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed across tissues; slightly induced by nitrogen deficiency; increased in ggp1 mutant under sulfur deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein enzyme assay and kinetics; expression profiling by qRT-PCR; inference from mutant compensatory expression
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential backup target for engineering glutathione turnover, but likely redundant and dual-purpose
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely affects both glutathione degradation and defense-metabolite conjugate processing
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGP3 recombinant protein degraded GSH in vitro with substantial catalytic efficiency. 2. GGP3 expression is constitutive and higher in mature organs, consistent with a role in GSH turnover. 3. The paper proposes that GGP3 likely assists GGP1 in cytosolic glutathione degradation and may compensate under sulfur deficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct mutant phenotype for a single ggp3 line was tested in this paper; role is inferred from biochemical activity and expression pattern.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclotransferase-mediated cleavage of glutathione to yield 5-oxoproline and Cys-Gly
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly degrades target product glutathione, especially under sulfur deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key condition-dependent control step under sulfur deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major sulfur storage and redistribution metabolite
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GGCT2;1 increases glutathione under sulfur deficiency but not under normal or nitrogen-deficient conditions, indicating a sulfur-deficiency-specific role in GSH degradation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Very low in most organs under normal growth; strongly induced under sulfur deficiency (~31-fold)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low constitutive expression; strongly induced by sulfur deficiency and after BSO in ggp1 background
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast complementation; knockout mutant metabolite measurements; double mutant BSO time course; qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Widely targeted metabolomics (LC-QqQ-MS/MS); no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for tuning sulfur-deficiency-responsive glutathione remobilization without strongly affecting basal GSH turnover
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter sulfur starvation responses and root/developmental programs under sulfur deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGCT2;1 complemented yeast GSH-utilization mutants. 2. ggct2;1 mutants accumulated more GSH specifically under sulfur deficiency, unlike normal conditions. 3. In a ggp1/ggct2;1 double mutant, GSH degradation after BSO treatment was slower than in wild type, demonstrating redundancy with GGP1. 4. GGCT2;1 expression rose strongly during sulfur deficiency, supporting a specialized stress-responsive role.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: GGCT-mediated degradation is less energy efficient than GGP-mediated degradation because 5-oxoproline must be converted to Glu using ATP.
